message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #2 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: get_checkpointer creates and registers a new ConnectionPool on every call with no per-conn_str pool reuse — repeated calls (e.g. per study run) leak pools, each holding a live worker thread and a min_size connection
DIMENSION: concurrency & load
SEVERITY: LOW
FILE: src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py 183-191, 211-212
DESCRIPTION: The once-per-process dedup (_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS) covers only the schema setup()/advisory-lock bootstrap, not pool creation. Every successful get_checkpointer() call constructs a fresh ConnectionPool (min_size=1, max_size=8) and appends it to _OPEN_POOLS via _register_pool_cleanup, with no lookup/reuse of an existing pool for the same conn_str. Each pool eagerly opens (open=True) and spins up a background maintenance worker thread plus at least one live Postgres connection. If callers invoke configured_checkpointer()/get_checkpointer() repeatedly — for example once per study run under sustained load rather than caching a single checkpointer — the process accumulates an unbounded set of pools, worker threads, and DB connections that are never closed until interpreter exit (_close_all_pools runs only at atexit). This is unbounded resource growth under load (thread and connection exhaustion over a long-lived server process). It is partly mitigated if callers cache the checkpointer, but the factory makes no such guarantee and the dedup structure suggests one-pool-per-conn_str was intended.
SUGGESTED FIX: Cache one open pool per conn_str (e.g. a dict keyed by conn_str guarded by _init_lock) and return a PostgresSaver over the existing pool on subsequent calls, instead of creating and registering a new pool every time. Close/evict cached pools that fail liveness. This bounds threads/connections at one pool per distinct conn_str regardless of call frequency.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Durable run-state checkpointer — local, data-sovereign (ADR-0003).
2	
3	The orchestration graph persists its full state (current phase, gate decisions, locked
4	hypotheses, verdicts) through a checkpointer so a run can interrupt at a human-approval gate
5	and resume later. That state holds the entire analysis, so the store is **on-box only**
6	(ADR-0003); a SaaS checkpointer is disqualified.
7	
8	This module is the LangGraph checkpointer factory, ported faithfully from the prior
9	platform's hardened implementation and generalized (dataset-agnostic ``ASSAY_*`` env, no
10	config-file coupling — see ADR-0006). The hardened behaviors are deliberately preserved
11	because each prevents a real production failure:
12	
13	- a **self-healing ConnectionPool** (not a raw connection): when Postgres goes away — node
14	  sleep, restart, idle timeout, a network blip — a cached raw connection is permanently
15	  broken and every later checkpoint op raises, i.e. durability is lost exactly during the
16	  interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to support. The pool runs a liveness check on
17	  checkout and replaces dead connections;
18	- a **session advisory lock** serializing first-time schema setup across *processes*, run on
19	  the *same* connection as the DDL (a pool would otherwise check out a different connection,
20	  leaving the lock ineffective);
21	- a **once-per-process-per-conn guard** (``setup()`` is idempotent but re-locking + replaying
22	  the migration query on every call is wasteful), with an in-process lock making the
23	  check-and-set atomic so concurrent threads don't both run setup;
24	- **credential redaction**: a backend exception (psycopg/psycopg_pool) can embed the full
25	  ``user:password`` URI/DSN in its own message, so the conn string is scrubbed precisely and
26	  the exception text via URI-userinfo + DSN-password backstops; the redacted message is then
27	  raised *outside* the exception handler, so the credential-bearing original is retained as
28	  neither ``__cause__`` nor ``__context__`` (a logger walking ``__context__`` cannot
29	  re-expose it);
30	- a single shared **atexit** pool-cleanup handler (one per process, not one per call) so the
31	  pool's worker thread is joined before interpreter finalization.
32	
33	LangGraph/psycopg are optional (the ``persistence`` extra) and imported lazily; the
34	credential-redaction and connection-resolution logic is pure and unit-tested offline.
35	"""
36	
37	from __future__ import annotations
38	
39	import atexit
40	import os
41	import re
42	import threading
43	from typing import Any, cast
44	from urllib.parse import urlsplit
45	
46	from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri
47	
48	_DEFAULT_PG = "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay"
49	
50	# Arbitrary fixed key for the Postgres session advisory lock that serializes checkpoint
51	# schema migrations across concurrent initializers. The value is irrelevant beyond being a
52	# stable per-logical-schema constant; "ASSY" as an int.
53	_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY = 0x4153_5359
54	
55	# Guards the process-local one-time-init bookkeeping. The PG advisory lock serializes DDL
56	# across PROCESSES; this lock makes the in-process check-and-set sequences (atexit
57	# registration, the initialized-conn set) atomic so two threads can't double-register or
58	# double-setup.
59	_init_lock = threading.Lock()
60	
61	# Pools opened by the factory, closed once at process exit via a single shared atexit handler
62	# (registering one handler per call would accumulate handlers across repeated factory calls).
63	_OPEN_POOLS: list[Any] = []
64	_atexit_registered = False
65	
66	# conn_strs whose schema bootstrap (advisory lock + setup() DDL) has already run in this
67	# process — bootstrap once per process per schema; the advisory lock still guards the
68	# first-time cross-process race.
69	_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS: set[str] = set()
70	
71	# Strips ``://user:password@`` (URI userinfo) from any URI-like substring. '/' is allowed
72	# inside the userinfo so a password containing '/' is still redacted; only '@' (the userinfo
73	# terminator) and whitespace bound the match.
74	_CREDS_RE = re.compile(r"://[^@\s]+@")
75	# Strips a libpq keyword/value DSN password token (``password=...`` / ``pgpassword=...``),
76	# handling quoted values that may contain spaces.
77	_DSN_PW_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)\b(password|pgpassword)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
78	
79	
80	def _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str: str) -> str:
81	    """Return ``scheme://host[:port]/path`` with any ``user:password`` userinfo stripped.
82	
83	    Robust to malformed userinfo: a password containing ``/``/``#`` makes ``urlsplit``'s
84	    ``.port`` raise ``ValueError`` on access, so the whole parse (including attribute access)
85	    is guarded and falls back to the generic userinfo-strip regex / a constant placeholder.
86	    """
87	    try:
88	        u = urlsplit(conn_str)
89	        host = u.hostname or ""
90	        port = u.port
91	        scheme = u.scheme
92	        path = u.path
93	    except ValueError:
94	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str)
95	    if not host:
96	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str) if scheme else "<store>"
97	    if ":" in host:  # IPv6 literal — urlsplit drops the brackets; restore them
98	        host = f"[{host}]"
99	    netloc = f"{host}:{port}" if port else host
100	    return f"{scheme}://{netloc}{path}" if scheme else "<store>"
101	
102	
103	def redact_creds(text: str, conn_str: str = "") -> str:
104	    """Remove credentials a backend exception may embed in its message text.
105	
106	    Layers, in order of precision:
107	
108	    1. exact replacement of the known ``conn_str`` (handles any password characters, incl.
109	       ``/`` and spaces) — this is the precise layer and covers the real connection string;
110	    2. a generic URI-userinfo strip (``://user:pass@`` → ``://``) as a backstop for any
111	       *other* URI in the text;
112	    3. a libpq DSN ``password=``/``pgpassword=`` strip for the keyword/value form.
113	
114	    Layers 2–3 are best-effort for *secondary* endpoints (e.g. a replica DSN) the caller did
115	    not pass as ``conn_str``; the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely.
116	    """
117	    if conn_str:
118	        text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))
119	    text = _CREDS_RE.sub("://", text)
120	    return _DSN_PW_RE.sub(r"\1=***", text)
121	
122	
123	def get_postgres_connection_string() -> str:
124	    """Resolve the Postgres connection string (``ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`` env → loopback default).
125	
126	    The env path bypasses any external config validation, so loopback is enforced here at the
127	    point of use (ADR-0003 defense-in-depth): graph state must not leave the box.
128	    """
129	    url = os.environ.get("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL") or _DEFAULT_PG
130	    return require_local_uri(url, what="checkpointer connection")
131	
132	
133	def _safe_close(pool: Any) -> None:
134	    """Close one pool, swallowing errors so a single failing ``close()`` does not abort
135	    cleanup of the remaining pools at interpreter exit (``close()`` calls a C ``finish()``
136	    that can raise)."""
137	    try:
138	        pool.close()
139	    except Exception:
140	        pass
141	
142	
143	def _close_all_pools() -> None:
144	    for p in _OPEN_POOLS:
145	        _safe_close(p)
146	
147	
148	def _register_pool_cleanup(pool: Any) -> None:
149	    global _atexit_registered
150	    with _init_lock:
151	        _OPEN_POOLS.append(pool)
152	        if not _atexit_registered:
153	            atexit.register(_close_all_pools)
154	            _atexit_registered = True
155	
156	
157	def get_checkpointer(use_memory: bool = False) -> Any:
158	    """Return a configured LangGraph checkpointer.
159	
160	    ``use_memory=True`` returns an in-memory saver (tests / no-Postgres). Otherwise a
161	    Postgres-backed saver over a self-healing pool, with advisory-locked one-time schema
162	    setup. Raises ``RuntimeError`` (with credentials redacted) if the backend is unreachable
163	    or the extra is not installed.
164	    """
165	    try:
166	        if use_memory:
167	            from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
168	
169	            return MemorySaver()
170	        from langgraph.checkpoint.postgres import PostgresSaver
171	        from psycopg.rows import dict_row
172	        from psycopg_pool import ConnectionPool
173	    except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
174	        raise RuntimeError(
175	            "Postgres checkpointer requires the 'persistence' extra "
176	            "(langgraph-checkpoint-postgres, psycopg[binary,pool]) — not installed"
177	        ) from exc
178	
179	    conn_str = get_postgres_connection_string()
180	    failure: str | None = None
181	    pool: Any = None
182	    try:
183	        pool = ConnectionPool(
184	            conn_str,
185	            min_size=1,
186	            max_size=8,
187	            kwargs={"autocommit": True, "prepare_threshold": 0, "row_factory": dict_row},
188	            check=ConnectionPool.check_connection,  # liveness check on checkout (self-healing)
189	            open=True,
190	        )
191	        _register_pool_cleanup(pool)
192	        # Bootstrap the schema once per process per conn_str. The advisory lock and the DDL
193	        # must run on the SAME connection (pg_advisory_lock is session-scoped); a pool-backed
194	        # setup() would lock one connection and DDL on another, leaving the lock ineffective.
195	        with _init_lock:
196	            if conn_str not in _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS:
197	                with pool.connection() as conn:
198	                    conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_lock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
199	                    try:
200	                        # row_factory=dict_row makes this connection dict-rowed at runtime
201	                        # (what PostgresSaver requires); the cast asserts that runtime truth
202	                        # the type system can't infer from the pool kwargs.
203	                        PostgresSaver(cast(Any, conn)).setup()  # DDL on the locked connection
204	                    finally:
205	                        # Best-effort unlock; the lock auto-releases when the connection
206	                        # returns to the pool, and raising here would mask a setup() error.
207	                        try:
208	                            conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
209	                        except Exception:
210	                            pass
211	                _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS.add(conn_str)
212	        return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, pool))  # dict-rowed pool (row_factory=dict_row)
213	    except Exception as exc:
214	        # Build the redacted message here, but raise it OUTSIDE the handler (below) so the
215	        # original — credential-bearing — exception is retained as neither __cause__ NOR
216	        # __context__ (a logger walking __context__ would otherwise re-expose the password).
217	        failure = (
218	            f"Postgres checkpointer connection failed (is Postgres running on "
219	            f"{_sanitize_conn_str(conn_str)}?): {redact_creds(str(exc), conn_str)}. "
220	            "For tests without Postgres, use get_checkpointer(use_memory=True)."
221	        )
222	        # The pool may have opened before bootstrap failed — close it and drop it from the
223	        # cleanup registry so a failed init does not leak the connection/worker thread (#105).
224	        if pool is not None:
225	            _safe_close(pool)
226	            with _init_lock:
227	                if pool in _OPEN_POOLS:
228	                    _OPEN_POOLS.remove(pool)
229	    raise RuntimeError(failure)
230	
231	
232	def configured_checkpointer(use_memory: bool | None = None) -> Any:
233	    """Return the checkpointer the environment selects.
234	
235	    ``use_memory`` (or ``ASSAY_CHECKPOINT_BACKEND=memory``) chooses the in-memory saver;
236	    otherwise Postgres.
237	    """
238	    if use_memory is None:
239	        use_memory = os.environ.get("ASSAY_CHECKPOINT_BACKEND", "postgres").lower() == "memory"
240	    return get_checkpointer(use_memory=use_memory)
241

content.text: The factory is only re-exported, not called anywhere within the engine itself. Let me check the full repo including tests and any orchestration/run entry points to understand the real usage pattern.

content.text: There's no production caller within the engine that invokes the factory per-run — it's a library, and `compile_graph` takes the checkpointer as a parameter. Let me check the existing tests around the dedup/pool behavior (lines 425-495 of test_persistence.py) to see what the pass-1 hardening actually asserted.

352	def fake_pg(monkeypatch):
353	    """Inject fake langgraph/psycopg modules so get_checkpointer runs the full hardened path
354	    offline, and reset the module's process-local bootstrap state."""
355	    import sys
356	    import types
357	
358	    from assay_engine.persistence import checkpoint as cp
359	
360	    state = {"setup_on": [], "lock_on": [], "pools": [], "atexit_registered": 0}
361	
362	    class FakeConn:
363	        def execute(self, sql, params=None):
364	            if "pg_advisory_lock" in sql:
365	                state["lock_on"].append(id(self))
366	
367	    class FakePool:
368	        check_connection = staticmethod(lambda *a, **k: None)
369	
370	        def __init__(self, conn_str, **kw):
371	            self.conn_str = conn_str
372	            self._conn = FakeConn()
373	            state["pools"].append(self)

375	        def connection(self):
376	            conn = self._conn
377	
378	            class _Ctx:
379	                def __enter__(self_):
380	                    return conn
381	
382	                def __exit__(self_, *a):
383	                    return False
384	
385	            return _Ctx()
386	
387	        def close(self):
388	            pass
389	
390	    class FakeSaver:
391	        def __init__(self, arg):
392	            self.arg = arg
393	
394	        def setup(self):
395	            state["setup_on"].append(id(self.arg))
396	
397	    def _mod(name, **attrs):
398	        m = types.ModuleType(name)
399	        for k, v in attrs.items():
400	            setattr(m, k, v)
401	        monkeypatch.setitem(sys.modules, name, m)
402	        return m
403	
404	    _mod("langgraph")
405	    _mod("langgraph.checkpoint")
406	    _mod("langgraph.checkpoint.postgres", PostgresSaver=FakeSaver)
407	    _mod("langgraph.checkpoint.memory", MemorySaver=type("MemorySaver", (), {}))
408	    _mod("psycopg")
409	    _mod("psycopg.rows", dict_row=object())
410	    _mod("psycopg_pool", ConnectionPool=FakePool)
411	
412	    # reset process-local bootstrap bookkeeping
413	    monkeypatch.setattr(cp, "_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS", set())
414	    monkeypatch.setattr(cp, "_OPEN_POOLS", [])
415	    monkeypatch.setattr(cp, "_atexit_registered", False)
416	    monkeypatch.setattr(
417	        cp,
418	        "atexit",
419	        types.SimpleNamespace(
420	            register=lambda fn: state.__setitem__(
421	                "atexit_registered", state["atexit_registered"] + 1
422	            )

449	def test_pool_closed_and_unregistered_when_bootstrap_fails(fake_pg):
450	    # #105: if schema bootstrap fails AFTER the pool opened, the pool must be closed and dropped
451	    # from the cleanup registry — not leaked.
452	    import sys
453	
454	    cp, state = fake_pg
455	    closed: list = []
456	    Pool = sys.modules["psycopg_pool"].ConnectionPool
457	    orig_close = Pool.close
458	    Pool.close = lambda self: (closed.append(self), orig_close(self))[1]  # record close
459	    Saver = sys.modules["langgraph.checkpoint.postgres"].PostgresSaver
460	    orig_setup = Saver.setup
461	
462	    def boom(self):
463	        raise RuntimeError("setup failed (schema DDL)")
464	
465	    Saver.setup = boom
466	    try:
467	        with pytest.raises(RuntimeError, match="connection failed"):
468	            cp.get_checkpointer()
469	        assert len(state["pools"]) == 1  # a pool was opened
470	        assert closed and closed[0] is state["pools"][0]  # and it was closed
471	        assert cp._OPEN_POOLS == []  # and removed from the cleanup registry (no leak)
472	    finally:
473	        Pool.close = orig_close
474	        Saver.setup = orig_setup
475

content.text:
This is very telling. The existing test `test_atexit_pool_cleanup_registered_once` (line 439-446) **explicitly asserts and enshrines the exact behavior the assessor flags as a defect**: two `get_checkpointer()` calls produce two pools (`len(state["pools"]) == 2`, `len(cp._OPEN_POOLS) == 2`). So the pass-1 hardening team deliberately chose "new pool per call" — the dedup is only for setup(), exactly as the assessor describes.

Now the key question is whether this is a *production* defect. The assessor's whole case hinges on "if callers invoke get_checkpointer() repeatedly per study run." Let me confirm the intended usage by checking the integration test that exercises the resume path and the graph module docs.

112	def test_postgres_state_durable_across_fresh_pool(monkeypatch):
113	    # the durability guarantee: state written through one pool is readable through a NEW pool
114	    # (it lives in postgres, not process memory) — what makes interrupt/resume survive restarts.
115	    from typing import TypedDict
116	
117	    from langgraph.graph import END, START, StateGraph
118	
119	    from assay_engine.persistence.checkpoint import get_checkpointer
120	
121	    monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL", _pg_url())
122	
123	    class S(TypedDict):
124	        n: int
125	
126	    def _graph(cp):
127	        b = StateGraph(S)
128	        b.add_node("inc", lambda s: {"n": s["n"] + 1})
129	        b.add_edge(START, "inc")
130	        b.add_edge("inc", END)
131	        return b.compile(checkpointer=cp)
132	
133	    try:
134	        cp1 = get_checkpointer(use_memory=False)
135	    except RuntimeError as exc:
136	        pytest.skip(f"postgres present but db not usable: {exc}")
137	    import uuid as _uuid
138	
139	    tid = f"durable-{_uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]}"
140	    cfg = {"configurable": {"thread_id": tid}}
141	    _graph(cp1).invoke({"n": 0}, cfg)
142	
143	    cp2 = get_checkpointer(use_memory=False)  # a fresh pool/saver over the same db
144	    persisted = _graph(cp2).get_state(cfg)
145	    assert persisted.values["n"] == 1  # state recovered from postgres, not memory

149	def test_postgres_concurrent_get_checkpointer(monkeypatch):
150	    # concurrent initializers must not race the advisory-locked one-time schema setup: the
151	    # _init_lock serializes the in-process check, the PG advisory lock the cross-process DDL.
152	    import threading
153	
154	    from assay_engine.persistence import checkpoint as cp
155	
156	    monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL", _pg_url())
157	    monkeypatch.setattr(cp, "_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS", set())  # force setup contention this run
158	
159	    results: list = []
160	    errors: list = []
161	
162	    def worker():
163	        try:
164	            results.append(cp.get_checkpointer(use_memory=False))
165	        except RuntimeError as exc:
166	            errors.append(exc)
167	
168	    threads = [threading.Thread(target=worker) for _ in range(8)]
169	    for t in threads:
170	        t.start()
171	    for t in threads:
172	        t.join(timeout=30)
173	
174	    if errors and not results:
175	        pytest.skip(f"postgres present but db not usable: {errors[0]}")
176	    assert not errors, f"concurrent get_checkpointer raised: {errors}"
177	    assert len(results) == 8  # every concurrent caller got a usable saver, no race failure

content.text:
Confirmed: `run_study` does not call `get_checkpointer`/`configured_checkpointer` at all. The factory is purely a public-API entry point that callers (operators building a graph via `compile_graph`) invoke explicitly and pass to `compile_graph`. There is no internal per-run loop calling it.

Let me write a quick PoC to definitively confirm the leak behavior under repeated calls, since the factual mechanism matters even if the trigger is hypothetical.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python -c "
import sys, types
from assay_engine.persistence import checkpoint as cp

# inject fakes (mirror the test fixture, minimal)
class FakeConn:
    def execute(self, sql, params=None): pass
class FakePool:
    check_connection = staticmethod(lambda *a, **k: None)
    instances = []
    def __init__(self, conn_str, **kw):
        FakePool.instances.append(self)
    def connection(self):
        c = FakeConn()
        class _Ctx:
            def __enter__(s): return c
            def __exit__(s,*a): return False
        return _Ctx()
    def close(self): pass
class FakeSaver:
    def __init__(self, arg): pass
    def setup(self): pass

def mod(name, **a):
    m = types.ModuleType(name)
    for k,v in a.items(): setattr(m,k,v)
    sys.modules[name]=m
mod('langgraph'); mod('langgraph.checkpoint')
mod('langgraph.checkpoint.postgres', PostgresSaver=FakeSaver)
mod('langgraph.checkpoint.memory', MemorySaver=type('M',(),{}))
mod('psycopg'); mod('psycopg.rows', dict_row=object())
mod('psycopg_pool', ConnectionPool=FakePool)
import os
os.environ['ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL']='postgresql://localhost:5432/assay'

for _ in range(50):
    cp.get_checkpointer()
print('pools created:', len(FakePool.instances))
print('_OPEN_POOLS tracked (never closed until atexit):', len(cp._OPEN_POOLS))
print('_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS (setup dedup):', cp._INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS)
"
input.description: PoC: repeated factory calls accumulate pools

content.text:
PoC confirms the mechanism exactly: 50 calls → 50 pools, all retained in `_OPEN_POOLS` (each with a real worker thread + min_size=1 live connection in production), never closed until atexit. The setup() dedup correctly fires once. So the assessor's factual description is 100% accurate.

Now the judgment. The factual mechanism is real, but the production-readiness severity hinges on whether anything actually calls it in a loop:

1. No internal engine code calls the factory per-run. `run_study` doesn't touch it. `compile_graph` receives the checkpointer as a parameter.
2. The factory is a public API entry point. Operators are expected to create one checkpointer and pass it to `compile_graph` (as every test and the integration tests do — `cp1`/`cp2` are created deliberately once each).
3. The existing pass-1 test `test_atexit_pool_cleanup_registered_once` explicitly *enshrines* "two calls → two pools" as intended behavior, and `test_postgres_state_durable_across_fresh_pool` deliberately creates a fresh pool per call to test durability — so multiple-pools-per-conn_str is a designed capability, not an oversight.

This is a real latent footgun: the factory makes no caching guarantee, and a naive operator who calls `configured_checkpointer()` once per study run on a long-lived server *would* leak pools/threads/connections unboundedly. That's a genuine, if conditional, production concern. The severity is correctly LOW — it requires a specific misuse pattern, no current caller triggers it, and pools are eventually reclaimed at process exit. The assessor's recalibration to LOW is right. It is not a false positive: the suggested fix (cache one pool per conn_str) is a reasonable, low-risk robustness improvement, and the dedup structure genuinely does suggest one-pool-per-conn_str was the original intent for the bootstrap path.

One nuance worth noting against severity: caching the pool would change the durability-test semantics (the "fresh pool over same db" test deliberately wants separate pools), but that's a test-fixture concern, not a blocker for the production fix.

input.reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL, severity correctly LOW. The factual mechanism is exactly as described and I reproduced it. get_checkpointer() constructs a fresh ConnectionPool(min_size=1, max_size=8, open=True) on every successful call (checkpoint.py:183-190) and appends it to _OPEN_POOLS via _register_pool_cleanup (191), with no lookup/reuse for the same conn_str. The only dedup, _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS (196,211), covers solely the schema setup()/advisory-lock bootstrap — not pool creation. Pools are closed only at atexit via _close_all_pools (143-145). Each production pool eagerly opens a Postgres connection and a background maintenance worker thread. So N repeated calls for the same conn_str accumulate N pools / N worker threads / N+ live DB connections retained until interpreter exit — unbounded growth if a caller invokes the factory per study run on a long-lived process.

This is a genuine latent footgun, NOT a false positive: the public factory offers no caching guarantee, and a naive operator calling configured_checkpointer() per-run on a long-lived server would exhaust threads/connections. The pass-1 hardening team explicitly enshrined "new pool per call" — test_atexit_pool_cleanup_registered_once asserts two calls => two pools — so this is intended-as-is and was not caught by pass 1 (the assessor is right that it is a gap pass 1 missed, not a re-report of a fixed item).

Severity is correctly LOW, for two reasons that bound real-world impact: (1) NO current code path triggers it — the composed runner run_study (pipeline.py) does not call the factory at all, compile_graph receives the checkpointer as a parameter, and every test/integration site creates the checkpointer deliberately once. The "per study run under sustained load" trigger is hypothetical caller misuse, not present behavior. (2) Resources are reclaimed at process exit, and one pool already self-heals, so a correctly-caching caller has bounded usage. It is a robustness/API-contract hardening item, not an active production failure. The suggested fix (cache one open pool per conn_str under _init_lock, evict on liveness failure) is reasonable and low-risk; note it would require updating the durability test that deliberately opens a second fresh pool over the same db.
input.evidence: PoC (engine venv, fake psycopg/langgraph injected per the repo's own test fixture): 50 sequential get_checkpointer() calls for one conn_str produced `pools created: 50`, `_OPEN_POOLS tracked (never closed until atexit): 50`, while `_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS` held exactly one entry — proving setup() deduped once but pool creation did not, and all 50 pools (worker thread + min_size=1 connection each in production) leak until atexit. Code: src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:183-191 (pool created+registered every call), 196/211 (dedup only guards setup), 143-154 (_OPEN_POOLS closed only at atexit). Intent-as-is evidence: tests/test_persistence.py:439-446 asserts two calls => len(pools)==2 and len(_OPEN_POOLS)==2; tests/integration/test_persistence_live.py:143 deliberately opens "a fresh pool/saver over the same db". No per-run caller: grep shows the factory is called only from configured_checkpointer and tests; src/assay_engine/pipeline.py (run_study) never references the checkpointer factory.

content.text:
Confirmed the finding. Summary:

The pass-2 finding is **TRUE** and severity **LOW** is correct.

Mechanism (verified by PoC): `get_checkpointer()` in `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py` builds a fresh `ConnectionPool` (open=True, min_size=1, worker thread) on every call (lines 183-191) and registers it in `_OPEN_POOLS`, which is only drained at atexit (lines 143-154). The `_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS` dedup (lines 196, 211) guards only `setup()`/the advisory lock, never pool creation. 50 calls → 50 leaked pools in my repro.

Why it's real but LOW:
- No current code path triggers it. `run_study` (pipeline.py) never calls the factory; `compile_graph` takes the checkpointer as a parameter; every test creates it deliberately once. The "per study run under load" trigger is hypothetical caller misuse.
- The behavior is intentional-as-is from pass 1 — `tests/test_persistence.py:439-446` explicitly asserts two calls → two pools, and the integration test at `test_persistence_live.py:143` deliberately opens a second fresh pool over the same DB. So this is a gap pass 1 left open, not a re-report.
- Resources reclaim at process exit; a caller that caches one checkpointer is unaffected.

It's a genuine API-contract/robustness footgun worth the suggested fix (cache one pool per conn_str under `_init_lock`), but not an active production failure. Note the fix would require updating the durability test that intentionally opens a second pool.
